title: What an American thinks about El Salvador

transcript.text: Hello, what's an American thinks of El Salvador?
I was in El Salvador like in the year of 2000 for like a week.
El Salvador is a pretty place.
Lots of hills, lots of volcanoes, friendly people.
Remember we were hanging out in the plaza one day.
This friendly lady started talking to us.
Um, pretty good roads.
I rode my bike around.
Um, I was there for an earthquake, so I guess there's lots of earthquakes.
I was staying in this room, my hotel room.
It was I guess perched on top of like the water tower.
Because it was, it was this one room that was up above everything else.
And I could see all over the city, it was really cool.
Um, but um, that night I felt an earthquake.
I felt it shaking.
I was like, whoa.
There was supposed to be good surfing there, but I didn't see any good surfing.
But it's a big, big, from the capital of El Salvador, San Salvador.
I rode my bike down and it goes way down.
Whee!
Really big descent.
It's, it's right next to the ocean, but it's way, way, way up there.
Like a couple thousand feet above sea level.
Um, town itself is pretty.
Surrounded by some nice hills.
Uh, lots of, um, nice jungle.
Oh, I did go to the city at first though and asked some kid like which direction it was to the downtown.
He was being a dick.
He like didn't want to tell me.
Um, but let's see.
Um, oh and then I, I took, I rode my bike because I was going to ride into Honduras.
But I got there and I couldn't pass because my, um, my, um, passport, my face was totally smudged off.
Because the passport they gave me.
When I got a new passport, I guess in Singapore, it, it didn't laminate it.
It just stuck a picture on it.
Um, so I rode up there and it was a big day of riding.
Like lots of climbing and stuff.
And then I went through this.
The last part was kind of like abandoned.
And it was like really wide road, paved, um, cement.
But there were like no houses around.
I guess they thought they were going to develop it.
But they never did or something.
I don't know.
But anyways, I took the bus back.
Because I didn't want to ride that same route again.
And I was sitting in the bus.
And there was this kid.
Like hobbling.
Like walking.
Like he was crippled.
Like with his legs down.
And moving his knees together.
Down the, on his knees down the aisle.
Like begging for money.
So I gave him money.
Then he just stood up and walked away.
I was like, what?
I looked at this other El Salvadorian next to me.
And they were like, I don't know.
I guess.
So yeah.
First time I'd ever seen it.
So blatant like that.
Um, let's see.
Um, there was, I remember seeing a rich person's section.
They had this kind of, the rich people off in their own section.
And, uh, they, they had some really nice houses.
So there's some people who live quite well over there.
But yeah, lots, it's a pretty place itself.
Lots of hills.
Um, hold on.
No, it's actually not.
A lot of it was, uh, clear cut.
That's what I remember.
Really clear cut.

Yeah, it's not a nice place.

So that's too bad, actually.
And the capital city is not that big.
It's kind of like a smaller capital city.
